items.title: Run only approved named entrypoints, never arbitrary script paths
items.description: The C# bridge must dispatch work by a small set of registered logical worker names that map, internally, to fixed module entrypoints shipped with the application. It must never accept a caller-supplied file path, module string, or '-c' code snippet and execute it. The mapping from name to entrypoint is a server-side allowlist.
items.applicability: Every invocation of a Python worker from the C# orchestration layer.
items.example: Bridge.RunAsync("embed-text", payload) resolves to the pinned module workers.embed_text; a request for path C:/tmp/anything.py or name 'os.system' is rejected before any process spawns.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the name-to-entrypoint table in code or signed config, validated at startup, not in user input. Test implication: assert that an unknown worker name and any path-like name both return a structured error and spawn no process. Generator implication: generated callers reference worker names only, never paths. Honest boundary: an allowlist constrains which entrypoints run, not what an approved entrypoint does internally, so each entrypoint still needs its own input validation.

items.title: JSON-in / JSON-out over stdin and stdout
items.description: The bridge passes a single JSON request object to the worker on stdin and reads a single JSON response object from stdout. stdout carries only the JSON envelope; all human-readable logging goes to stderr. The envelope has a stable shape with an explicit ok flag, a result payload, and an error block.
items.applicability: The wire contract for every worker task invoked across the C#-Python boundary.
items.example: C# writes {"task":"doc-extract","args":{...}} to stdin; the worker prints {"ok":true,"result":{...}} as the last line of stdout and logs progress to stderr.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: parse exactly one JSON document from stdout and treat trailing or interleaved non-JSON on stdout as a protocol violation. Test implication: feed a worker that prints stray text to stdout and assert the bridge flags a protocol error rather than mis-parsing. Generator implication: generated workers must reserve stdout for the envelope only. Honest boundary: a streaming or chunked task needs a framed protocol; this single-envelope contract is for discrete request/response work.

items.title: Enforce a hard timeout and kill the worker process
items.description: Every worker run carries a hard wall-clock timeout owned by the C# side. On expiry the bridge terminates the child process (and any process group it spawned) and returns a structured timeout error. A worker is never trusted to self-limit.
items.applicability: All worker invocations, especially long-running mining, scraping, or model-adjacent tasks.
items.example: A pattern-mine run with a 120 s budget that stalls is killed at 120 s; the bridge returns {"ok":false,"error":{"kind":"timeout"}} and the orchestrator continues.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: kill the whole child process tree, not just the parent, or grandchildren may leak. Test implication: launch a deliberately hanging worker and assert it is killed within the budget and no zombie remains. Generator implication: generated callers always supply or inherit a timeout; none is unbounded. Honest boundary: a kill can leave partial side effects on disk, so workers must write atomically (see the safe-working-directory and idempotency items).

items.title: Confine each run to a safe working directory
items.description: Each worker run executes with its current directory set to a dedicated, application-owned scratch directory passed in by the bridge. Inputs are staged in and outputs are read back from that directory. The worker treats this directory as the only place it may freely create files.
items.applicability: Any worker that reads staged inputs or emits intermediate or output files.
items.example: The bridge creates runs/{runId}/ as the working directory, drops input.json there, and collects output files from it after the run, then deletes it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: create the scratch directory per run and clean it afterwards so runs cannot read each other's residue. Test implication: assert two concurrent runs use distinct directories and cannot collide. Generator implication: generated workers resolve all relative paths against the provided working directory. Honest boundary: a working directory is a convention, not a kernel sandbox; pair it with the no-writes-outside-approved-folders rule to actually constrain a misbehaving worker.

items.title: No writes outside approved folders
items.description: A worker may write only inside its run-scoped working directory and any explicitly approved output folder the bridge grants. Writes elsewhere (system paths, the repository, user home, sibling project folders) are disallowed and, where the platform supports it, blocked by least-privilege filesystem permissions.
items.applicability: Every worker that produces output, caches, or temporary files.
items.example: An extract-knowledge worker writes proposals only to the approved proposals output folder; an attempt to write into the application binaries directory fails by permission and is reported as an error.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: prefer OS-level permission scoping over honour-system path checks, but keep path-prefix validation as defence in depth. Test implication: assert a worker attempting an out-of-bounds write fails and surfaces a structured error. Generator implication: generated workers accept output locations as parameters and never hardcode absolute system paths. Honest boundary: full filesystem confinement needs OS sandboxing; application-level checks reduce but do not eliminate risk from a compromised dependency.

items.title: No network access unless explicitly allowlisted
items.description: Workers run with networking off by default. A worker that legitimately needs the network (for example the web-scrape task) receives an explicit domain allowlist and rate policy from the bridge; all other workers must function fully offline and must not silently reach out to the internet.
items.applicability: All workers; especially the boundary between pure-compute workers and the scraping worker.
items.example: embed-text and code-mine run with no network; web-scrape runs only against the configured allowlisted documentation domains and refuses any host not on the list.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pass the allowlist as data and enforce it at the request layer inside the worker, ideally backed by an outbound proxy or firewall rule. Test implication: assert a non-network worker makes zero outbound connections and a scrape worker rejects a non-allowlisted host. Generator implication: generated workers declare whether they need network; the default is none. Honest boundary: in-process allowlist checks are bypassable by native code, so treat network policy as defence in depth, not a guarantee.

items.title: Capture stdout and stderr to a structured log
items.description: The bridge captures the worker's stdout envelope and full stderr stream and records them, with the worker name, run id, arguments digest, exit code, and elapsed time, to a structured log. Logs are retained for audit and debugging and are correlated to the orchestration request that triggered the run.
items.applicability: Operational diagnostics, audit, and post-incident analysis of worker behaviour.
items.example: A failed doc-extract run leaves a log line with runId, exitCode=1, the stderr traceback, and a redacted args digest, letting an operator reproduce the case.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: cap captured stderr size and stream it rather than buffering unbounded memory. Test implication: assert that a worker emitting a large stderr volume is logged within the size cap and does not exhaust memory. Generator implication: generated workers log diagnostics to stderr, never to stdout. Honest boundary: logs may contain input excerpts, so apply the redaction and no-secrets rules before persisting them.

items.title: Return errors as structured JSON, never throw across the bridge
items.description: A worker must catch its own failures and emit a structured error envelope ({"ok":false,"error":{"kind":...,"message":...}}) rather than letting an exception escape as an uncaught crash. The C# side maps a missing or malformed envelope, or a non-zero exit, to a typed bridge error so the orchestrator handles failure as data, not as an unhandled host exception.
items.applicability: The failure path of every worker invocation.
items.example: A build-dataset worker hitting a malformed input row returns {"ok":false,"error":{"kind":"bad-input","message":"row 14 missing field"}}; the orchestrator records it and proceeds with the remaining batch.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: wrap the worker body in a top-level handler that converts any exception into the error envelope, and still treat a process crash as a synthetic transport error on the C# side. Test implication: assert that both a worker-raised error and a hard crash produce a typed result the caller can branch on. Generator implication: generated workers funnel all returns through one envelope builder. Honest boundary: a process killed for timeout or OOM cannot emit an envelope, so the bridge must always have a fallback transport-error mapping.

items.title: Detect Python availability gracefully and report unavailable
items.description: Python is an optional capability. The bridge probes for a usable interpreter once, caches the result, and exposes an IsAvailable signal. When Python is absent the bridge returns a clean 'unavailable' status for worker requests and the orchestrator falls back to its deterministic non-Python path. Absence must degrade behaviour, never crash the application or fail its tests.
items.applicability: Application startup, capability gating, and every worker-dependent feature.
items.example: On a machine with no Python, Bridge.IsAvailable is false; a feature that would call embed-text instead uses the C# deterministic fallback and the page still renders.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make the C# bridge unit tests pass with Python absent by asserting the unavailable path, not by requiring an interpreter. Test implication: a test run on a Python-free build agent must stay green. Generator implication: generated features always check availability before dispatching a worker. Honest boundary: a present interpreter is not a working one; also verify required version-pinned packages import before declaring a worker usable.

items.title: Maintain a fixed worker task taxonomy
items.description: Python work is organised as a closed set of named task types: code-mine, pattern-mine, doc-extract, web-scrape, embed-text, rerank, build-dataset, graph-enrich, and extract-knowledge. Each name has a defined input shape, output shape, and resource profile. New work joins the taxonomy by adding a reviewed entry, not by inventing ad hoc invocations.
items.applicability: Designing, routing, and governing every Python-side capability.
items.example: A request to summarise a repository's call graph is routed to graph-enrich; there is no generic 'run this code' task.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: bind each taxonomy entry to one approved entrypoint and one schema so routing stays total and auditable. Test implication: assert each declared task has a schema and entrypoint and that an undeclared task is rejected. Generator implication: generators target taxonomy names, easing reuse across projects. Honest boundary: the taxonomy is deliberately conservative; genuinely new needs require human review to extend it rather than overloading an existing task.

items.title: Use Python for library-rich tasks; keep C# as orchestration and safety authority
items.description: Python is chosen for tasks where its ecosystem is materially stronger and faster to iterate on: embeddings, NLP, AST and code mining, and scraping. C# remains the authority for orchestration, policy enforcement, persistence, and the approval lifecycle. The Python side is a stateless compute tool; it never owns workflow decisions or the system of record.
items.applicability: Deciding where a capability should live in the platform.
items.example: Tokenisation and embedding computation run in a Python worker, but whether the resulting vectors are stored, and the approval state of any derived knowledge, are decided and persisted by C#.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pass only the data a worker needs and treat its output as untrusted input to be validated on return. Test implication: assert the orchestrator re-validates worker output rather than trusting it. Generator implication: generated designs route durable state and decisions to C#, computation to Python. Honest boundary: this split adds an IPC boundary and serialization cost, so very small tasks may be cheaper to do directly in C#.

items.title: Make workers idempotent and deterministic where possible
items.description: A worker should produce the same output for the same input and be safe to retry. Outputs are written atomically (temp file then rename) and keyed by an input digest so a re-run overwrites cleanly rather than appending. Where true determinism is impossible, the worker fixes random seeds and records the non-deterministic factors in its output.
items.applicability: Retryable batch tasks, caching, and reproducible knowledge generation.
items.example: build-dataset writes to dataset.{digest}.tmp then renames to dataset.{digest}.json; a retry after a timeout reproduces the identical file.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never append to a shared output in place, since a killed run could leave it half-written. Test implication: run a worker twice on identical input and assert byte-identical or schema-equivalent output. Generator implication: generated workers expose an input digest so callers can cache. Honest boundary: model-backed tasks (embeddings, reranking) are only deterministic given a fixed model and seed, so record the model identity as part of the key.

items.title: Pin worker dependencies to exact versions
items.description: Worker requirements are pinned to exact versions (and ideally hashes) in a checked-in requirements file per worker set. The bridge verifies the resolved environment matches the pin before declaring workers usable, so a silently upgraded library cannot change output or reintroduce a vulnerability.
items.applicability: Worker environment provisioning and availability gating.
items.example: requirements.txt pins each package to an exact version; on startup the bridge checks the installed versions and marks Python unavailable with a clear message if they drift.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the pin file under review and update it deliberately, not opportunistically. Test implication: assert the availability probe fails closed on a version mismatch. Generator implication: generated worker sets ship with their own pinned manifest. Honest boundary: pinning aids reproducibility but ages; periodic deliberate review is still required to take security fixes.

items.title: No secrets in the worker environment
items.description: Workers run with a minimal, scrubbed environment. Connection strings, API keys, and Data-Protection material are never passed as environment variables or arguments to a worker. A worker that needs a credential receives a narrowly-scoped, short-lived token for one specific operation, never the platform's own secrets.
items.applicability: Every worker spawn and its environment construction.
items.example: An embed-text worker inherits no DB connection string and no key directory path; it receives only the text to embed and returns vectors.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build the child environment from an explicit allowlist of variables, not by inheriting the parent's full environment. Test implication: assert the spawned environment contains none of the application's secret variables. Generator implication: generated workers never read ambient secrets. Honest boundary: process arguments and environments can be visible to local administrators, so even scoped tokens must be short-lived and minimally privileged.

items.title: Validate worker input and output against a schema on both sides
items.description: Each task declares a request and response schema. The worker validates the request it receives and the C# side validates the response it gets back. Neither side trusts the other's payload blindly, so a malformed or oversized envelope is rejected with a typed error instead of propagating bad data into the system of record.
items.applicability: The serialization boundary for every worker task.
items.example: doc-extract rejects a request missing the document field; the bridge rejects a response whose chunks array exceeds the declared maximum size.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep request and response schemas versioned alongside the taxonomy entry. Test implication: assert that malformed input is rejected by the worker and malformed output is rejected by the bridge. Generator implication: generators emit schema-conformant payloads on both sides. Honest boundary: schema validation catches shape errors, not semantic correctness, so a separately reviewed business check is still needed for high-stakes output.

items.title: Bound worker resource use and concurrency
items.description: The bridge caps how many worker processes run concurrently and bounds each run's memory and output size. Heavy tasks are serialized or queued rather than spawned without limit, so a burst of work cannot exhaust the host. The cap is configuration, with a conservative default.
items.applicability: Scheduling and admission control for worker invocations under load.
items.example: With a concurrency cap of two, a third embed-text request waits in a queue rather than spawning a third interpreter and thrashing memory.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the cap in the bridge, not in callers, so it cannot be bypassed. Test implication: submit more requests than the cap and assert the excess queues rather than over-spawning. Generator implication: generated callers submit work and await admission rather than spawning directly. Honest boundary: a single worker can still over-allocate within its own limit, so per-run memory and output caps remain necessary alongside the concurrency cap.

items.title: Workers are stateless tools, not the system of record
items.description: A worker holds no durable state between runs and is not a source of truth. Any output it produces becomes a proposal or an intermediate artefact that the C# side persists, versions, and subjects to the approval lifecycle. Restarting or replacing a worker must not lose authoritative data because the worker never held any.
items.applicability: Designing the boundary between transient computation and durable, governed knowledge.
items.example: extract-knowledge emits candidate knowledge items; they enter the database as unapproved proposals and only become installed knowledge after human approval, exactly as any other proposal source.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat all worker output as untrusted, unapproved input until persisted and reviewed by the C# pipeline. Test implication: assert worker output cannot enter the approved knowledge set without passing the approval path. Generator implication: generated flows route worker output through the proposal lifecycle, never directly to approved storage. Honest boundary: statelessness simplifies recovery but means any needed caching must be made explicit and owned by C#, not hidden inside a worker.
